participants.name: A (l65njz)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-l65njz9l
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-l65njz9l
participants.professional_persona: A retired teacher now volunteering with a non-profit that uses AI to make educational content accessible for students with learning disabilities. I help design and test inclusive AI tutors.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I have decades of classroom experience and a deep understanding of special education needs. I also have basic coding skills from an AI literacy course for seniors, which helps me provide user feedback to developers.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to see every special education school in Singapore adopt at least one AI tool within five years. I also hope to inspire other seniors to engage with AI for social good.

participants.name: A (d1a8ht)
participants.professional_persona: A machine learning engineer who transitioned from a traditional software engineering role via a Career Conversion Programme. He now works at a logistics SME to optimise route planning using AI.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in Python, TensorFlow, and cloud-based AI services. Experienced in applying off-the-shelf AI solutions to operational pain points.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to specialise in resource-efficient AI and contribute to making AI more accessible for SMEs across Singapore.

participants.name: Dr. (c9viyd)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Tan is a senior lecturer at Singapore Polytechnic who bridges academia and industry by running AI skilling programs for mid-career workers. He helps companies scope AI projects under the 100E programme.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Broad applied AI skills including computer vision, NLP, and MLOps. Adept at translating business problems into AI solution roadmaps and teaching non-tech professionals.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Goal is to create a replicable AI apprenticeship model that upskills 500 workers in three years. Hopes to see his trainees lead AI transformation in their SMEs.

participants.name: A (1pmopb)
participants.professional_persona: A research scientist turned AI practitioner who collaborates with public researchers through the 100 Experiments programme. He develops custom AI solutions for the healthcare sector.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of natural language processing and computer vision, with a track record of publishing at top conferences. Skilled in bridging research and real-world deployment.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to translate cutting-edge AI research into commercial products that improve patient outcomes, and to become a recognised thought leader in AI for good.

participants.name: A (rsa6z1)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-rsa6z16i
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-rsa6z16i
participants.professional_persona: A mid-career professional in supply chain management, I pivoted into AI after completing a company-led training programme under TeSA. Now I lead AI adoption pilots at a logistics SME, focusing on demand forecasting and route optimisation.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I have hands-on experience with predictive analytics, process automation, and the AI Readiness Index (AIRI). I also mentored two colleagues through their own AI upskilling paths.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I aim to become a digital transformation lead in my company and eventually drive a sector-wide AI consortium for logistics, helping smaller firms adopt AI responsibly for public good.

initial_posts.narrative: Based on the policy context, the government has committed over S$500 million for AI R&D through AI Singapore, which I take as the total funding for the NAIS 2.0 period. The 100 Experiments programme offers co-funding up to S$330,000 per project, as stated. For compute infrastructure, given that most compute goes to industry and the need to support many experiments, I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours available in the initial phase, reflecting a modest but meaningful allocation. For trainers, my experience with skilling programs suggests that a dedicated team of about 50 AI experts and trainers is plausible to guide enterprises and practitioners, balancing quality and scale.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so I anchor total funding at S$500 million.
The 100 Experiments programme provides 'co-funding of up to S$330,000 per project', which I take as the maximum per project.
Given the emphasis on compute for industry and the scale of 100E, an initial allocation of 500,000 GPU hours is a reasonable estimate for the first year.
Based on my role running skilling programs, a team of 50 trainers can effectively support dozens of enterprises and trainees simultaneously.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, I estimate 80 enterprise outreach sessions to engage SMEs like my employer, leveraging my experience that such sessions are key to getting problem statements. With 500,000 GPU hours and a focus on pairing, I foresee 20 matchmaking events to connect researchers with enterprise challenges, as this aligns with the 100E programme's goal. Training workshops should be 30, given the need to upskill practitioners across sectors, and platform onboarding assists at 150, reflecting the technical support needed for SMEs to use AI tools effectively. These estimates are grounded in the resource constraints and my firsthand knowledge of AI adoption challenges in logistics.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, I estimate 60 enterprise outreach sessions. In my experience running skilling programs, engaging SMEs requires sustained, targeted outreach—not just mass events. With 500,000 GPU hours and the need to pair researchers with enterprise challenges, I foresee 15 matchmaking events, as each event requires significant preparation and follow-up to yield quality pairings. Training workshops should be 25, reflecting the capacity of 50 trainers to deliver hands-on, impactful sessions without diluting quality. Platform onboarding assists at 120, based on my work helping SMEs adopt AI tools—each onboarding is time-intensive, and 120 is a realistic number given the trainer count and enterprise engagement. I note that AIPractitioner estimated higher numbers (80 outreach, 20 matchmaking, 30 workshops, 150 assists), but my experience suggests that quality over quantity is critical for the 100E programme's success; overstretching resources could lead to shallow engagements and poor project outcomes.
evidence: As a senior lecturer running AI skilling programs, I've found that effective enterprise outreach requires multiple touchpoints per SME, so 60 sessions is a realistic number given 50 trainers and the need for depth over breadth.

Matchmaking events are resource-intensive; with 500,000 GPU hours and a focus on quality pairings, 15 events allow for thorough preparation and follow-up, aligning with my experience in scoping 100E projects.
Training workshops must be hands-on and small-group to be effective; 25 workshops from 50 trainers ensures each workshop has adequate support, based on my own program delivery experience.
Platform onboarding is time-consuming for SMEs new to AI; 120 assists is a feasible target given the trainer capacity and the need to provide meaningful technical support, not just cursory help.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, I estimate 50 enterprise outreach sessions. From my volunteer work with SMEs in special education, I know that meaningful outreach to small enterprises requires personalized, small-group sessions to build trust and identify real problem statements—not large-scale events. With 500,000 GPU hours and the need for quality pairings, I foresee 12 matchmaking events, as each event demands careful preparation and follow-up to ensure researchers understand niche challenges like inclusive education. Training workshops should be 20, reflecting the 50 trainers' capacity to deliver hands-on, sector-specific workshops without diluting quality—my experience with teacher training shows that 20 workshops can reach about 400 practitioners effectively. Platform onboarding assists at 100, based on the technical support needed for SMEs to use AI tools; each onboarding is time-intensive, and 100 is realistic given the trainer count and the need for sustained assistance. I note that AIPractitioner estimated higher numbers (80 outreach, 20 matchmaking, 30 workshops, 150 assists), but my experience with resource-constrained non-profits suggests that overstretching leads to shallow engagements. Dr. AIResearcher's estimates (60 outreach, 15 matchmaking, 25 workshops, 120 assists) are closer to mine, but I lean lower because the 100E programme's focus on no-commercial-solution problems requires deeper, more customized outreach to small enterprises, which reduces the total number of sessions possible.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed how enterprise outreach, matchmaking, training, and onboarding activities drive the 100E programme's approval and deployment targets, emphasizing that quality of engagement is critical given limited trainers (50) and compute resources (500,000 GPU hours). There was consensus that personalized, small-group sessions are essential for identifying viable problem statements and ensuring effective researcher-enterprise pairings. Disagreement centered on the feasible scale of activities: estimates ranged from 50 to 80 outreach sessions, 12 to 20 matchmaking events, 20 to 30 training workshops, and 100 to 150 onboarding assists, with a central tendency around 60-70 outreach, 14-18 matchmaking, 22-28 workshops, and 110-140 assists. The divergence reflected differing views on whether to prioritize breadth (higher numbers) or depth (lower numbers) given resource constraints.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the inputs of S$500 million total funding, S$330,000 co-funding cap per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, and the activities of 55 outreach sessions, 14 matchmaking events, 22 training workshops, and 110 onboarding assists, I estimate that about 30 100E projects will be approved in 2023. This is because the outreach and matchmaking activities are designed to identify viable problem statements from enterprises, but the approval process is rigorous and only a subset of proposed projects will meet the criteria for no-commercial-solution problems and have strong researcher-enterprise pairings. From my experience, many SMEs need multiple touchpoints before committing, so the conversion rate from outreach to approved projects is modest. For AI prototypes, I expect 20 to be successfully built, as each approved project takes time to develop and test, and some may face technical or resource constraints. The 500,000 GPU hours provide ample compute for experimentation, but prototype development is iterative and not all projects will reach a working prototype within the year. For trainees, I estimate 400 will complete the programme, based on the 22 training workshops reaching about 440 practitioners (assuming 20 per workshop) and a 90% completion rate, which aligns with my mentoring experience where most trainees finish when given hands-on support. For enterprises with at least one AI solution, I estimate 25, as some enterprises may have multiple projects or prototypes, but the focus on quality over breadth means only a subset will have a deployable solution by year-end. My estimates are lower than what an optimistic industry participant might suggest, but they reflect the realistic pace of AI adoption in SMEs, where trust-building and customization take time.
evidence: From my experience leading AI adoption at a logistics SME, only about half of the enterprises that attend outreach sessions and matchmaking events end up submitting a viable 100E proposal, and of those, roughly 60% get approved, leading to about 30 approved projects from 55 outreach sessions and 14 matchmaking events.

Based on my own prototype development experience, each approved project takes 3-6 months to build a working prototype, and with 30 approved projects, about 20 will have a prototype within 2023 due to resource constraints and technical challenges.
The 22 training workshops, each with about 20 participants, can train 440 individuals, and with a 90% completion rate based on my mentoring experience, 400 trainees will complete the programme.

From my company's experience, not every prototype leads to a deployed solution; about 80% of prototypes are adopted by the enterprise, so 20 prototypes yield 16 enterprises with solutions, but some enterprises may have multiple solutions, so I estimate 25 enterprises with at least one solution.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the inputs of S$500M total funding, S$330K co-funding cap per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, and the activities of 60 outreach sessions, 15 matchmaking events, 25 training workshops, and 120 onboarding assists, I estimate 25 approved 100E projects. evidence: From my experience scoping 100E projects with SMEs, only about 40% of matched enterprises submit a viable proposal, and of those, roughly 60% pass the rigorous approval process, yielding ~25 approved projects from 15 matchmaking events.

They agreed that prototype development is constrained by 6-9 month timelines and technical challenges, yielding 15-20 prototypes, and that trainee completion rates are lower than 90% due to mid-career work demands, with estimates from 280 to 400 (central tendency ~350). initial_posts.narrative: From my experience running AI skilling programs and scoping 100E projects with SMEs, I estimate that 25 projects were approved in 2023, consistent with the prior phase's outputs. However, deployment into production is a slower process: many prototypes face integration challenges, data quality issues, or require further refinement before going live. Based on my work with SMEs, only about half of completed prototypes reach production within the same year, so I estimate 12 deployed projects. The AI practitioner pool is growing but likely still below the 15,000 target; my programs and others have trained around 350 completions, and with other initiatives, the total pool might be around 12,000. The AI Trailblazers programme aimed for 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days, but in practice, many enterprises need more time to develop meaningful use cases, so I estimate 80. Enterprise AI adoption is increasing but still modest; from my interactions, about 18.5% of enterprises have adopted AI solutions, as many SMEs are still in early experimentation stages.
